preamble.summary: Nebuchadnezzar's madness — the tree dream. This is one of the most dramatically different chapters between the OG and the MT/Theodotion. The OG is substantially shorter and rearranges the narrative. Theodotion follows the MT closely. The OG omits Nebuchadnezzar's first-person narration and presents the story in the third person.
preamble.notable_variants: The OG version is roughly one-third shorter than the MT/Theodotion. It lacks the royal edict framework (vv. 1-3 in MT), condenses the dream interpretation, and frames the madness episode differently. The OG names the 18th year of Nebuchadnezzar's reign as the setting (absent from MT). Theodotion preserves the MT's first-person royal proclamation structure.
preamble.structural_notes: MT and Theodotion have 37 verses. The OG is considerably shorter with different verse boundaries. We annotate against the 37-verse MT structure, noting where the OG diverges substantially.

verses.variant_rendering: OG omits the royal edict opening entirely
variant_notes: The MT/Theodotion frame chapter 4 as a royal edict from Nebuchadnezzar in first person. The OG omits this framework and presents the narrative in third person throughout.
OG omits. The MT's doxological opening ('How great are his signs...') appears only in Theodotion.
OG omits the conclusion of the edict opening. The OG begins its narrative directly with the dream.

verses.variant_rendering: OG: 'In the eighteenth year of his reign'
verses.mt_rendering: I was at ease in my house
variant_notes: The OG adds a specific chronological note (18th year) absent from the MT. It also shifts to third person.

Both agree on the image, but the OG's description of the tree is more concise than the MT/Theodotion.
Substantively identical, but OG condenses the surrounding description.
OG abbreviates the tree's description. The MT's poetic elaboration of beasts finding shade and birds nesting is shorter in the OG.

verses.mt_rendering: a watcher and a holy one
variant_notes: The OG replaces the distinctive Aramaic 'watcher and holy one' (ir v'qaddish) with the generic 'angel.' Theodotion preserves the Aramaic idiom. The Watcher terminology connects to 1 Enoch and is unique to Daniel in the MT canon.
No significant variant between the LXX/Theodotion and the MT for this verse.
No significant variant between the LXX/Theodotion and the MT for this verse.

verses.lxx_reading: ἡ καρδία αὐτοῦ ἀπὸ τῶν ἀνθρώπων ἀλλοιωθήσεται
verses.variant_rendering: his heart will be changed from among men
verses.mt_rendering: let his heart be changed from that of a man
variant_notes: OG and Theodotion agree on the substance. The transformation from human to beast-mind is consistent across all three traditions.
All versions agree on 'seven periods/times.' The exact duration is debated — seven years is the traditional interpretation.
No significant variant between the LXX/Theodotion and the MT for this verse.

OG condenses the interpretation scene. In the MT/Theodotion, Daniel hesitates for an hour, troubled. The OG shortens the emotional response.

verses.variant_rendering: The tree... you are, O king
verses.mt_rendering: You are the tree, O king
variant_notes: Both Greek versions preserve the dramatic identification. The OG is slightly more direct.

The madness episode is told differently in the OG. The OG states that Nebuchadnezzar was driven from his kingdom and 'they confined him in chains and shackles' — language absent from the MT, which simply says he was driven from men.

verses.lxx_reading: αἱ φρένες μου ἐπ᾽ ἐμὲ ἐπεστράφησαν
variant_notes: Substantively identical. The OG is terser in describing the restoration.
OG omits most of the restoration thanksgiving. The MT/Theodotion have an extended doxology; the OG jumps directly to the summary.
No significant variant between the LXX/Theodotion and the MT for this verse.

OG has a substantially different conclusion. It adds a notice about Nebuchadnezzar writing an 'encyclical letter' to all nations — framing the chapter as a decree — but in third person, unlike the MT's first-person framework.

verses.variant_rendering: I praise and I highly exalt
variant_notes: The closing doxology is present in all three versions but the OG frames it differently. Theodotion follows the MT closely.
